chunks.heading: What is the first thing a survivor needs to do, and what information should be included?
chunks.text: The first thing a survivor needs to do is send the Fund a death notification, by email to unjspf-deathrelated@un.org, providing: the deceased's full name; date of birth; date of death (day/month/year); Pension Fund reference number (UNJSPF Unique ID or Retirement number), if known; and official mailing address. If possible, also include: contact details for the surviving family; an original or certified copy of the death certificate (sent as soon as possible); and any additional helpful information. After receiving the notification, the Fund will stop payments to the retiree and check if any overpayment needs to be recovered.

chunks.heading: Who is entitled to a surviving spouse's benefit?
chunks.text: This benefit is payable to the surviving spouse of a retiree who was entitled to a retirement, early retirement, deferred retirement or disability benefit, if married to the retiree at the date of separation from service and remained married until the date of death; or to the surviving spouse of a participant who died in service, if married at the date of death. Even if these conditions are met, in certain limited cases a surviving spouse's benefit will not be payable — e.g., if the participant had previously elected a benefit option that eliminated the right to a survivor's benefit.

chunks.heading: Under what circumstances is a surviving spouse's benefit shared with another beneficiary?
chunks.text: If the Fund determines there are two or more surviving spouses entitled to a benefit, it is divided equally between them. If the Article 34 benefit is to be shared with one or more divorced surviving spouse(s) (Article 35 bis), the division is proportionate to the length of each survivor's marriage to the participant.

chunks.heading: How much is the surviving spouse's benefit?
chunks.text: Generally, the benefit is equivalent to half of the full retirement, early retirement or disability benefit payable to the participant or retiree before any lump sum calculation. In the case of a full deferred retirement benefit not yet in payment, the surviving spouse's benefit is equivalent to half the actuarial value of the full deferred retirement benefit at the time of the retiree's death.

chunks.heading: When does payment of a surviving spouse's benefit start?
chunks.text: Effective as of the first day of the month following the date of the retiree's death; in the case of a participant's death in service, it is due as of the day after death.

chunks.heading: How can a surviving spouse apply for the benefit?
chunks.text: The Fund will require, as applicable: death certificate of the participant/retiree; birth certificate(s) for each entitled survivor; marriage certificate(s) as applicable; divorce certificate(s) as applicable; original authenticated Payment Instructions (form Pens.E/2-B) from each survivor claiming a benefit; a copy of an official identification document bearing the survivor's signature; and a recent bank statement matching the survivor's name and account details. Include the participant's/retiree's Unique Identification Number (UID) with all documents.

chunks.heading: How is the surviving spouse's benefit paid and for how long is it payable?
chunks.text: Paid monthly to the bank account specified by the beneficiary, for as long as the surviving spouse is alive.

chunks.heading: Who can apply for a divorced surviving spouse's benefit?
chunks.text: The former spouse of a participant or retiree who survives them can request this benefit.

chunks.heading: What conditions have to be met for the payment of a divorced surviving spouse's benefit?
chunks.text: The deceased died in service or was entitled to a retirement, early retirement, deferred retirement or disability benefit at death; the couple was married for a continuous period of at least ten years during which contributions were paid to the participant's UNJSPF account; death occurred within 15 years of the final divorce, unless the participant was under obligation to pay spousal maintenance; the former spouse is at least 40 years old; and there was no renouncement of UNJSPF pension benefit entitlements.

chunks.heading: How much is the divorced surviving spouse's benefit?
chunks.text: If separation from service occurred before 1 April 1999, the benefit is a fixed amount (estimated at US$10,961.04 per year / US$913.42 per month as of 1 April 2018), capped at the amount payable to a surviving spouse. If separation occurred on or after 1 April 1999 and there is one or more surviving spouse(s) entitled to a benefit, it is divided in proportion to the duration of marriages. If separation occurred on or after 1 April 1999 and there is no surviving spouse entitled, the divorced surviving spouse's benefit equals half the full benefit payable to the participant or retiree.

chunks.heading: When does payment of a divorced surviving spouse's benefit start?
chunks.text: If separation occurred before 1 April 1999, payment is due as of the first day of the month following the participant's/retiree's death or 1 April 1999, whichever is later. If separation occurred on or after 1 April 1999, payment is due as of the first day of the month following the UNJSPF Chief Executive of Pension Administration's decision authorizing the entitlement.

chunks.heading: How can the divorced surviving spouse apply for the benefit?
chunks.text: The required documentation matches that for a surviving spouse's benefit (death certificate, birth/marriage/divorce certificates, original authenticated Payment Instructions form Pens.E/2-B, ID document, bank statement) — remember to include the Unique Identification Number (UID).

chunks.heading: Who is considered a "spouse married after separation," and who can purchase an annuity for them?
chunks.text: A person who marries a retiree after the retiree's date of separation from service is a "spouse married after separation." The election to purchase an annuity for such a spouse must be made by the retiree, who must be in receipt of a periodic benefit (retirement, early retirement, or deferred retirement benefit in payment).

chunks.heading: What process must a retiree follow to purchase an annuity for a spouse married after separation?
chunks.text: The retiree submits form PENS.E/3-A within one year of the marriage date to express interest; the Fund provides an estimate. If the retiree decides to proceed, they submit form PENS.E/3-B to confirm, and payment is made via a monthly deduction from the retiree's benefit, reducing it for life.

chunks.heading: Does the entitlement become effective immediately upon electing to purchase the annuity?
chunks.text: No. Both the reduction in the retiree's monthly benefit and the spouse's entitlement to a prospective survivor's benefit become effective 18 months after the date of marriage.

chunks.heading: How is the entitlement for a spouse married after separation calculated?
chunks.text: The retiree chooses the annuity amount as a percentage of their own benefit entitlement; the monthly benefit payable to the spouse cannot exceed the amount of the retiree's reduced monthly benefit.

chunks.heading: When does payment of the benefit begin, and can the retiree opt out?
chunks.text: Once effective, the benefit is payable as of the first day of the month following the retiree's death. The retiree may opt out of purchasing the annuity at any time before the election takes effect (18 months after the marriage date); once in effect, it cannot be revoked except by divorce or death of the spouse married after separation.

chunks.heading: Who is entitled to a child's benefit, and who counts as a "child"?
chunks.text: This benefit is payable to each child of a participant entitled to a retirement, early retirement or disability benefit, or who died in service, while the child remains under 21, and also to a child over 21 found by the relevant Staff Pension Committee to be incapacitated for substantial gainful employment. "Child" means the natural, legally adopted or step-child of a participant existing on the date of separation or death in service, including a child conceived before that date but born after ("in utero"). Only one child's benefit is paid per child even if both parents are UNJSPF beneficiaries. A child's benefit is not payable if the participant opted for a deferred retirement benefit or withdrawal settlement.

chunks.heading: How much is the child's benefit, and are there limits?
chunks.text: The annual amount is one-third of the participant's benefit, subject to a minimum estimated at US$1,829.28 per year and a maximum estimated at US$3,638.40 per year (as of 1 April 2018). Irrespective of the number of children, Article 36(f) caps the total the Fund can pay for all of a participant's children at US$10,915.20 as of 1 April 2018.

chunks.heading: Under what circumstances is an increased child's benefit payable?
chunks.text: Subject to verification, a higher child's benefit under Article 36(e) may be paid to the child of a deceased beneficiary (participant, surviving spouse, divorced surviving spouse) should the child become an orphan.

chunks.heading: When does a child's benefit commence, and how long is it payable?
chunks.text: Payment generally starts at separation from service or upon reaching normal retirement age, whichever is later, or at death in service. It is payable monthly until age 21, unless the child is found incapacitated for substantial gainful employment, in which case it continues for as long as the child remains so disabled.

chunks.heading: Can the child's benefit be paid to someone other than the UNJSPF retiree/beneficiary, or directly to the child?
chunks.text: Yes, payment can be made to the custodial parent (if not the retiree/beneficiary) or to the child's legal guardian if both parents are deceased. It may also be paid directly to the child if over age 16 and able to open a bank account, by submitting: form PF.23 (Change in Payment Instructions) with the child's original signature; form PENS.S3 (Signature Specimen of Child Aged 16 Years or Older); a copy of a valid passport or other official government ID; and a bank document confirming the banking information.

chunks.heading: Who is a "secondary dependant," and when is this benefit payable?
chunks.text: A secondary dependant is a participant's/retiree's mother or father or unmarried sister or brother under age 21, who was financially dependent on the participant/retiree during service or from separation until death, meaning in receipt of financial support sufficient to meet the criteria for a secondary dependant's allowance under the employing organization's Staff Regulations and Rules, whether or not actually paid. The benefit is payable to not more than one surviving secondary dependant, and only if there is no surviving spouse, divorced surviving spouse or child eligible for a periodic benefit. It is not payable if a benefit is or was ever payable to a child or spouse of the participant, and (for a brother or sister) not payable if the participant elected a deferred retirement benefit.

chunks.heading: How much is the secondary dependant's benefit?
chunks.text: If payable to a surviving parent, it equals the rate payable for a surviving spouse. If payable to a surviving sister or brother, it equals the rate payable for a child, payable until age 21 unless the dependant is disabled, in which case it can continue beyond 21.

chunks.heading: When is a residual settlement payable?
chunks.text: A residual settlement becomes payable if: (a) the participant died in service with no survivors entitled to a benefit under Articles 34, 35 bis, 35 ter, 36 or 37; (b) the participant died after service but before electing a benefit, with five or more years of contributory service and no entitled survivors; (c) the participant died during entitlement to a retirement, early retirement, deferred retirement or disability benefit, with no further benefits payable to survivors, and the total benefits already paid were less than the participant's own contributions plus interest; or (d) the participant had entitled survivors and, following the death of the participant and the death or termination of entitlement of all survivors, the total amount paid by the Fund is less than the participant's own contributions plus interest. The Fund pays a residual settlement to the designated beneficiary, equivalent to the participant's own contributions with compound interest minus any payments already made. It is payable to the person or institution designated on the A2 form; a new designation must be completed for each new participation and supersedes previous designations. If there is no valid A2 form on file, the amount is paid to the estate.

chunks.heading: What actions should participants, retirees and survivors take regarding records and documents?
chunks.text: Before retirement: ensure records with your employing organization are up to date regarding marital status and dependants, and submit birth certificates, marriage certificates, divorce decrees, and child information at the time of separation. After retirement: update marital status or family composition changes promptly with supporting records. In the event of death: notify the Fund as soon as possible via email at unjspf-deathrelated@un.org or the website Contact Us form, to avoid payment delays or overpayments, following the document list under the Surviving Spouse's Benefit section. Medical constraints: if unable to sign documents for medical reasons, a fingerprint can be used if authenticated by a UN official or government authority; however, a thumbprint cannot be used to change payment instructions or address for a beneficiary incapable of managing financial affairs — a legal guardian must be officially appointed instead.

chunks.heading: What ongoing requirements apply once a survivor's benefit is in payment?
chunks.text: You will receive an Entitlement Letter containing all payment details once a benefit is processed. Each year you must submit a proof-of-life Certificate of Entitlement (CE), either through the Digital CE app (DCE) or by mailing the paper form, to verify continuous eligibility. Submit a signed PF.23 form to the Fund via mail or Member Self-Service immediately if banking coordinates or addresses change, to prevent disbursement issues.
